triage_context.big_picture: We run a large Angular front‑end (presentation layer) that is part of a multi‑container system serving internal and external users via a web portal. The UI is built with Angular 18 LTS, Angular CLI 18, Webpack 5 and a custom SASS styling pipeline. The upcoming Angular 19 release removes the old SASS loader and requires the new Angular‑Builder compiler. This task prepares the front‑end for the framework upgrade, preventing build breakage and visual regressions that would affect all users of the portal. The migration is timed with the broader Angular 19 upgrade, so delaying it would block the framework update and increase technical debt.
triage_context.scope_boundary: IN: All build‑time configuration files (angular.json, tsconfig.json, any custom webpack config), SASS import statements, style‑library packages, CI/CD build scripts that invoke the Angular build, and related unit‑test configuration (Karma, Playwright) that compile styles. OUT: Application business logic, backend services, domain‑layer components, existing UI component code that does not touch SASS imports, and any non‑Angular parts of the system.

context_boundaries.boundary: Angular 19 deprecates the legacy sass-loader and mandates the use of @angular-devkit/build-angular:application as the SASS compiler. All build scripts and angular.json entries that reference the old compiler must be updated, otherwise the build will fail or produce incorrect CSS.

Any npm packages that provide SASS utilities (e.g., node‑sass, sass, sass‑loader) may be incompatible with the new Angular‑Builder compiler. Their versions need to be verified and possibly upgraded before the migration.

The issue explicitly states that additional testing is required to minimise regression risk. All unit‑tests (Karma) and end‑to‑end tests (Playwright) that compile SASS must be re‑run after the migration.
source_facts: Issue description: "additional testing may be necessary to minimize the risk of potential regressions"

context_boundaries.boundary: The CI/CD pipeline currently invokes the Angular build via scripts that assume the old compiler. Those scripts must be updated to use the new builder and the pipeline must be validated on the supported Node/Gradle versions.
source_facts: tech_versions.json: Node version implied by Angular CLI 18

triage_context.architecture_notes: WALKTHROUGH: The front‑end lives in its own container (the *Frontend* container) and belongs to the **presentation** layer. The primary component involved is the **Angular Build System** (a configuration component that lives in the presentation layer but touches the infrastructure layer via CI scripts). It consumes style assets from the **Shared Style Library** component (also presentation) and produces compiled CSS that is bundled into the static assets served by the **Web Server** component in the *Infrastructure* container. Neighbouring components include the **Component Library** (Angular Material, CDK) and the **Theme Service** that provides runtime theming. The migration will only touch the build‑system component and its interfaces to the CI pipeline; all downstream UI components remain unchanged.

anticipated_questions.question: Do any SASS files need to be rewritten because of the new compiler?
anticipated_questions.answer: The new Angular‑Builder compiler drops support for the deprecated `@import` syntax that was allowed by the old loader. Review the ADR for the list of prohibited patterns and update any SASS files that use them.

anticipated_questions.question: Will the migration affect the CI/CD build process?
anticipated_questions.answer: Yes. The build scripts that call `ng build` must be updated to use the new builder configuration. Verify that the CI environment uses a Node version compatible with Angular 19 and that Gradle steps are unchanged.

anticipated_questions.question: Are there known compatibility issues with Angular Material or other UI libraries?
anticipated_questions.answer: Angular 19 is compatible with the latest versions of Angular Material and CDK. Ensure that the project upgrades those packages to the matching 19.x releases after the SASS migration.

anticipated_questions.question: Do we need to adjust unit‑tests or e2e tests?
anticipated_questions.answer: All tests that compile styles (Karma unit tests and Playwright e2e tests) should be re‑run after the migration. No test code changes are expected unless a test relies on the old import syntax.

anticipated_questions.question: What is the fallback if the migration breaks the build?
anticipated_questions.answer: Keep the previous `angular.json` configuration in version control. If the new build fails, you can revert to the old configuration and postpone the migration while investigating the cause.
